Synthetic Canadians: Cross-Dataset Comparison

Synthetic Survey Data Quality Across Five Canadian Political Surveys

Author

Synthetic Opinion Lab

Published

March 16, 2026

Overview

This report compares synthetic data quality across five Canadian political surveys spanning 2019–2021. Three generators are evaluated on each dataset: a Large Language Model (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct via Together API), an Item Response Theory model (Graded Response Model), and an Agent-based opinion dynamics simulation.

Quality Scores by Generator

LLM Consistently Leads

Key observations:

  • LLM is the top-performing generator on every dataset without exception.
  • IRT and Agent are closely matched across datasets, with IRT narrowly ahead on DC surveys and Agent slightly ahead on CES21.
  • DC surveys score higher than CES surveys for all generators. DC surveys use uniform 4-point scales for most items, while CES mixes 4/5/8/9-point scales — wider scale variety makes distribution matching harder.
  • DC21 is the easiest dataset (LLM = 0.803), likely due to its large sample (n = 7,576) and homogeneous scale structure.

Score Gap: LLM vs Best Alternative

Sample Sizes and Durations

Summary Table

Dataset Survey N real LLM IRT Agent Best
DC19 Democracy Checkup 2019 2,749 0.743 0.530 0.501 LLM
DC20 Democracy Checkup 2020 4,043 0.749 0.569 0.531 LLM
DC21 Democracy Checkup 2021 7,576 0.803 0.625 0.548 LLM
CES19 Canadian Election Study 2019 302 0.586 0.486 0.485 LLM
CES21 Canadian Election Study 2021 9,418 0.606 0.485 0.559 LLM

LLM = Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-Turbo via Together API (100 synthetic respondents) IRT = Graded Response Model (1,000 synthetic respondents) Agent = Opinion dynamics simulation, Watts-Strogatz network (500 synthetic respondents)
